==Geography==

Hawaii, also known as the Big Island, is the largest island in the Hawaiian archipelago and in the United States by total area. It lies in the central Pacific Ocean and forms the southeasternmost island of the state of Hawaii. The island is part of Hawaii County and is noted for its large size, varied climate zones, and dramatic volcanic landscapes. Hawaii is bounded by the Pacific Ocean on all sides and is separated from the other main Hawaiian Islands by channels of open water.

The island has an area of about 4,028 square miles (10,432 km²), making it larger than all of the other Hawaiian Islands combined. Its terrain ranges from sea level coastal plains to some of the highest elevations in the state. The island’s two most prominent mountains are Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa, both of which rise to great heights above sea level, while the seafloor beneath the island extends the total volcanic mass to far greater depths. The island includes a number of distinct districts and communities, with Hilo on the east side and Kailua-Kona on the west side serving as major population centers.

Hawaii’s coastline is highly irregular, with lava-generated cliffs, black and green sand beaches, coves, and bays. The island’s interior includes high volcanic slopes, alpine environments, and large areas of undeveloped land. Much of the land is protected in parks, reserves, and conservation districts, including Hawaii Volcanoes National Park and portions of the Hawaiian Islands Humpback Whale National Marine Sanctuary.

==Geology==

Hawaii is the youngest and largest island in the Hawaiian hotspot chain. It was formed by volcanism associated with the Pacific Plate moving northwestward over a relatively stationary mantle plume. As the plate has moved, successive volcanoes have formed to the southeast, making Hawaii the current focus of volcanic activity in the Hawaiian chain.

The island is built from five major shield volcanoes: Kohala, Mauna Kea, Hualālai, Mauna Loa, and Kīlauea. Kohala, located in the northwest, is the oldest of the five and is considered extinct. Mauna Kea and Hualālai are dormant, though both have geologic potential for future eruptions. Mauna Loa and Kīlauea remain active and are among the most closely monitored volcanoes in the world. Kīlauea has produced frequent eruptions in historical times, while Mauna Loa is one of the largest volcanoes on Earth by volume and surface area.

The island’s geology is dominated by basaltic lava flows that have created extensive shield structures and broad lava plains. The age of the island increases from southeast to northwest, reflecting the movement of the Pacific Plate over the hotspot. The southeastern side of the island, particularly around Kīlauea and the Puna district, is geologically youthful and frequently reshaped by eruptions. In contrast, the northwestern portions are older and more heavily eroded.

Volcanic activity continues to influence the island’s landscape. Eruptions have created new land, destroyed roads and settlements, and periodically altered coastlines. The island also features lava tubes, cinder cones, pit craters, and calderas. Among its most notable geological features are the summit caldera of Kīlauea, the summit region of Mauna Loa, and the high-altitude volcanic deserts of Mauna Kea.

The island’s mountains are also significant in terms of elevation above the ocean floor. Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa are among the tallest mountains in the world when measured from their submarine base to summit. Their immense mass has played a major role in shaping the island’s weather patterns and ecology.

==Climate==

Hawaii has one of the most varied climates of any island in the world, owing to its size, elevation range, and topography. The island includes tropical wet environments on windward slopes, dry leeward coasts, temperate uplands, and alpine conditions at high elevations. Trade winds from the northeast bring moisture to the eastern side of the island, while the western side is generally drier because of rain-shadow effects from the island’s major mountains.

Temperature at low elevations is typically warm year-round, with relatively small seasonal variation. Coastal areas often experience daytime highs in the tropical range, moderated by ocean breezes. At higher elevations, temperatures become substantially cooler, and frost or snowfall can occur on Mauna Kea and occasionally Mauna Loa during winter months.

Rainfall varies dramatically across the island. Hilo and surrounding areas on the windward side are among the wettest places in the United States, with frequent precipitation and lush vegetation. In contrast, parts of the Kona and Kohala coasts can be much drier, supporting more open landscapes and agricultural uses that depend on irrigation or localized rainfall. The island’s central and high-altitude regions receive enough moisture to sustain cloud forests, bogs, and other specialized habitats.

The island is also subject to occasional hurricanes, tropical storms, and droughts. Volcanic smog, known locally as vog, may affect air quality downwind of active eruptions from Kīlauea or Mauna Loa, particularly on the western and southern parts of the island.

==Ecology==

Hawaii contains a wide range of ecosystems, from coastal strand vegetation and dry forests to montane rain forests and alpine desert. The island’s ecological diversity is shaped by its steep rainfall gradients, elevation changes, and ongoing volcanic activity. Native plant and animal communities evolved in long isolation, producing many endemic species found nowhere else.

The island’s lower elevations originally supported dry forests, shrublands, and coastal habitats, while windward slopes contained extensive rain forests. Higher elevations on Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa supported montane ecosystems adapted to cooler temperatures and thinner soils. Lava flows periodically created new surfaces for ecological succession, beginning with lichens and mosses and gradually progressing to more complex plant communities over time.

Native fauna includes numerous endemic birds, insects, and plants, although many species have declined because of habitat loss, invasive species, disease, and human development. Introduced mammals such as rats, pigs, goats, and cattle have altered native ecosystems through predation and grazing. Mosquito-borne diseases, including avian malaria and avian pox, have also affected native forest birds, particularly at lower elevations.

Protected areas on the island preserve important habitats and biological communities. Hawaii Volcanoes National Park contains active volcanic landscapes as well as native forest and coastal ecosystems. Other conservation lands include forest reserves, watershed areas, and marine protected areas that safeguard coral reefs and nearshore environments. Coral reefs around the island support a rich marine ecosystem, although they are vulnerable to warming, pollution, and coastal development.

==History==

Hawaii was settled by Polynesians, likely arriving from the Marquesas Islands and later from Tahiti, beginning over a millennium ago. These settlers developed a complex society organized into chiefdoms, with agriculture, fishing, and religious practices adapted to the island’s diverse environments. The island of Hawaii held special significance in traditional Hawaiian culture and was associated with deities such as Pele, the volcano goddess.

By the late 18th century, the island became central to the unification of the Hawaiian Islands under King Kamehameha I, who was born on the island of Hawaii. After a series of campaigns beginning in the 1780s and 1790s, Kamehameha used the island as his base and eventually established a kingdom encompassing the entire archipelago. The island thus played a major role in the formation of the Kingdom of Hawaii.

European contact increased after the arrival of Captain James Cook in 1778, though Cook did not land on the island until 1779, when he was killed at Kealakekua Bay. Subsequent contact with Europeans and Americans introduced new trade networks, diseases, and political influences. During the 19th century, the island’s economy increasingly incorporated ranching, sugar cultivation, and commercial agriculture, alongside indigenous subsistence practices.

After the overthrow of the Hawaiian monarchy in 1893 and the annexation of Hawaii by the United States in 1898, the island became part of the U.S. territory of Hawaii. It later entered statehood in 1959 as part of the State of Hawaii. In the 20th and 21st centuries, the island developed into a center for tourism, astronomy, conservation, and scientific research, while also retaining significant agricultural and cultural importance.

Hawaii has also been shaped by major volcanic events in the modern era, including the prolonged eruptions of Kīlauea and Mauna Loa. These eruptions have periodically destroyed homes and infrastructure but have also reinforced the island’s status as one of the most geologically dynamic places on Earth.

==Demographics and settlements==

The island of Hawaii has a relatively small population compared with its large land area, resulting in low population density. Most residents live in communities along the coast, where conditions are more favorable for settlement and transportation. Hilo, on the northeastern coast, is the county seat and the largest population center. Kailua-Kona, on the western coast, is another major town and a focal point for tourism and commerce.

Other communities include Waimea, Pāhoa, Honokaʻa, Kealakekua, and Volcano, each with distinct geographic and cultural settings. Settlement patterns reflect both historical land use and the constraints imposed by volcanic terrain, steep slopes, and varying rainfall. Many inland and southeastern areas remain sparsely populated because of lava hazards, poor soils, conservation status, or remoteness.

The island’s population is ethnically diverse, reflecting centuries of migration and intermarriage among Native Hawaiians, Asians, Europeans, and people from elsewhere in the Pacific and North America. Hawaiian Pidgin is widely spoken in informal settings, alongside English and Hawaiian. Cultural traditions remain visible in place names, festivals, hula, and religious practices.

==Economy==

The economy of Hawaii is based on tourism, agriculture, government services, astronomy, and small-scale manufacturing. Tourism is concentrated mainly on the Kona coast, Hilo, and destinations associated with beaches, volcanoes, and scenic landscapes. Hawaii Volcanoes National Park is one of the island’s most visited attractions, drawing visitors to active volcanic terrain and native ecosystems.

Agriculture has long been important on the island. Historical sugar plantations and cattle ranching shaped large parts of the landscape, particularly in the 19th and 20th centuries. Although large plantation agriculture has declined, ranching remains significant, and the island produces crops such as coffee, macadamia nuts, tropical fruits, flowers, and other specialty products. The Kona district is especially known for coffee cultivation.

Astronomy and science-related activities are concentrated on the summits of Mauna Kea and elsewhere on the island, where high altitude, stable air, and limited light pollution provide favorable observing conditions. Several major observatories operate on Mauna Kea, making the island an important center for astronomical research. At the same time, development on sacred lands has been the subject of ongoing cultural and political debate.

==Culture and landmarks==

Hawaii is a major center of Native Hawaiian cultural identity, and its landscapes remain closely connected to traditional beliefs and practices. Many place names, stories, and ritual associations are tied to volcanic features, fishponds, valleys, and coastal sites. The island is home to sacred places including temples, burial sites, and regions associated with Pele and other deities.

Notable landmarks include Mauna Kea, Mauna Loa, Kīlauea, Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, the Puʻuhonua o Hōnaunau National Historical Park, Kealakekua Bay, and Waipio Valley. The island also contains historic churches, plantation-era buildings, and cultural sites reflecting the diverse communities that have lived there. Its natural landmarks are central to both tourism and local identity.

The island’s cultural life includes Hawaiian music, hula, surfing, canoeing, and community celebrations. Traditional knowledge of the land and sea continues to influence environmental stewardship and local land-use debates. Because of its combination of active volcanism, native ecosystems, and living cultural traditions, Hawaii holds a distinctive place in the broader history and geography of the Hawaiian Islands.

method1.generated_article:
==Geography and environment==
Hawaii is a U.S. state composed of islands, and its geography and environment are documented through official sources covering land and water areas, physical geography, climate, air and water quality, and related measurements.[1] The State of Hawaii Data Book identifies the U.S. Geological Survey, the National Ocean Survey, the National Climatic Data Center, the Hawaii State Department of Land and Natural Resources, the Hawaii State Department of Health, and the University of Hawaii at Mānoa as important sources for geographic and environmental data.[1] Detailed background information on the state’s physical setting is also provided in the *Atlas of Hawaii* and in the *State of Hawaii Data Book 2024*.[1]

==Health data and public health==
Hawaii’s health data resources include statewide and federal sources such as the Hawaii Department of Health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the National Center for Health Statistics.[2] Available tools and datasets cover topics including health trends, health care system profiles, state health facts, and women’s health indicators.[2] The Office of Hawaiian Affairs also created the Native Hawaiian Data Book to identify basic demographic data and needs of Native Hawaiians.[2] Additional Hawaii-focused health resources include the Hawaii Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Hawaii Health Information Corporation, and the Hawaii State Department of Health.[2]

Hawaii health data portals provide county-level and statewide indicators on immunizations, infectious diseases, survey results, and broader health outcomes.[3] The Hawaii Health Survey is described as a continuous statewide household survey of health and sociodemographic conditions, and the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse includes data from the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[3] Hawaii Health Matters also offers community snapshots and a Healthy People 2020 tracker for county-level comparisons.[3]

Health workforce and population studies in Hawaii use data from the Hawaii State Department of Health’s Hawaii Health Data Warehouse to analyze chronic disease, injury, infectious disease, and workforce trends.[4] These studies compare health indicators and worker characteristics to assess shortages, demand for care, and factors influencing the state’s health care industry.[4] The literature cited in these materials suggests that demand for health care services in Hawaii is likely to increase as the population changes and as some health indicators worsen.[4]

==Interstate and international relationships==
Hawaii has developed sister-state and friendship relationships with 18 states and prefectures around the world, including five in Japan.[5] The Japanese sister-state relationships listed include Fukuoka, Okinawa, Hiroshima, Ehime, and Hokkaido.[5] The Hawaii Global Education Foundation has reported work with partners in Hawaii and Fukuoka to honor notable figures and strengthen ties between Hawaii and Japan.[5]

==Etymology==
The name Hawaii is generally considered to come from Hawaiian Hawaiʻi, from Proto-Polynesian *hawaiki.[2] It has been interpreted as meaning “place of the gods” and as a reference to Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.[2] Another account says the name may derive from Hawaiki, the former name of Raiatea, regarded as the ancestral home of Polynesians.[1]

==Geography and environment==
Hawaii consists of eight major islands and 124 minor islands, with a total land area of 6,425 square miles and a general coastline of about 750 miles.[2][4] The major islands, from west to east, are Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, Maui, and Hawaii.[5] The state extends across a long volcanic island chain in the central Pacific Ocean, stretching roughly 1,500 miles from Kure Island in the west to the island of Hawaii in the east.[5] Honolulu is 214 miles from Hilo, 1,367 miles from Kure Atoll, 2,397 miles from San Francisco, and 4,829 miles from Washington, D.C.[2][4] The highest peak in the state is Mauna Kea, which rises 13,796 feet above sea level.[2][4] Other major geographic features include Kaukonahua Stream on Oahu, the state’s longest stream at 33 miles, Halalii on Niihau, the largest lake at 841 acres, and Kahiwa Falls on Molokai, the highest named waterfall at 1,750 feet.[2][4]

Much of the Hawaiian landscape was formed by volcanic activity as the Pacific Plate moved over a hotspot beneath the central Pacific Ocean.[5] The islands and seamounts of the chain began forming at least 70 million years ago, and volcanic activity remains active today at Mauna Loa and Kilauea on Hawaii, as well as at the Lō‘ihi Seamount southeast of the island.[5] Hawaii’s highest mountains are on the island of Hawaii, and the state’s topography includes dramatic sea cliffs, especially along the northeastern coast of Molokai.[4][5] The cliffs between Umilehi Point and Puukaoku Point rise to about 3,250 feet and have been described as among the highest sea cliffs in the world.[4]

Hawaii’s climate and environment vary widely across the islands, with rainfall ranging from very dry areas such as Kawaihae, South Kohala, to extremely wet areas such as the summit of Waialeale on Kauai.[4] The state’s geographic measurements have also been used to document water use, earthquakes, tsunamis, and noise levels, reflecting the importance of environmental monitoring in Hawaii.[4] Major sources for geographic and environmental data include the U.S. Geological Survey, the National Ocean Survey, the National Weather Service, and agencies of the State of Hawaii.[1][4]

==History==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, who arrived by navigating the Pacific using the stars, likely around the 5th century CE, though some traditions and historical summaries describe settlement as much earlier.[3][5] The islands later became the center of a Hawaiian Kingdom, with Kamehameha I uniting the islands in 1810 after a series of interisland conflicts.[3] In 1778, Captain James Cook became the first recorded European to contact the Hawaiian Islands, which he named the Sandwich Islands, and his arrival marked the beginning of sustained Western contact.[3]  

In 1819, Liholiho ended the traditional kapu system by eating with women during a feast, a symbolic act that helped dismantle the old religious and social order.[3] The first Christian missionaries arrived in 1820, and by the 1830s sugar plantations had begun to transform the islands’ economy, making agriculture a dominant force.[3] Lahaina served as the capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from 1820 to 1845.[3]  

During the 19th century, Hawaii’s population and labor system were reshaped by immigration and foreign economic interests, including Japanese emigration companies, attempts to recruit Chinese contract labor, and the growth of plantation society.[1] At the same time, scholars and observers recorded Hawaiian place names, language, religion, and social history, preserving important details of the islands’ cultural past.[1]  

Hawaii was annexed into the United States and eventually became the 50th U.S. state on August 21, 1959.[4] Modern historical interest in Hawaii also includes archaeology and geology, which have helped explain both the human history of the islands and their volcanic origin as part of the Hawaiian-Emperor chain.[2][5]

==Demographics==
Hawaii’s population was estimated at 1,441,553 in 2021, making it the 30th most populous U.S. state, and the state had grown by 25,681 people, or 1.8%, since 2019.[1] Most residents live on Oahu, which accounted for 69.4% of the state’s population, followed by Hawaii Island, Maui County, and Kauai County.[1]

Recent Census estimates show that Hawaii’s population continued to fluctuate in the early 2020s, with a 2024 estimate of 1,446,146 and a 2025 estimate of 1,432,820.[5] The state’s population was 1,455,271 at the 2020 census, up from 1,360,301 in 2010.[5]

Hawaii is one of the most ethnically diverse states in the country.[1] In 2024, Census estimates described the population as 25.1% White alone, 37.4% Asian alone, 10.4% Native Hawaiian and other Pacific Islander alone, 24.5% two or more races, and 10.2% Hispanic or Latino.[5]

Japanese and Chinese communities remain especially significant in Hawaii’s ethnic makeup.[1] In 2021, 11.6% of residents identified as Japanese alone and 6.0% as Chinese alone, while larger shares reported Japanese alone or in combination and Chinese alone or in combination.[1]

Native Hawaiians are central to the state’s demographic history and identity.[4] One analysis said Native Hawaiians made up about 6% of the islands’ population, or 21% when people of part-Hawaiian ancestry are included, and estimated that there were about 683,000 Native Hawaiians in 1778.[4]

Immigration also plays an important role in Hawaii’s population profile.[1] Based on the 2021 American Community Survey, Hawaii had 270,345 immigrants, or 18.8% of residents, the sixth-highest share among the states.[1]

Hawaii’s age structure is relatively older than the U.S. as a whole.[2][5] In 2024, the median age was 41.0 years, and people age 65 and over made up 18.0% of the population.[2] By the 2020–2024 period, Census estimates put the share of people 65 and over at 21.5%.[5]

The state’s gender balance is nearly even.[2][5] In 2024, women comprised 50.3% of the population and men 49.7%, while the 2020–2024 estimate put the female share at 49.9%.[2][5]

Hawaii also has high educational attainment and a diverse labor force.[5] Among residents age 25 and older, 93.2% were high school graduates or higher and 36.1% had a bachelor’s degree or higher in 2020–2024.[5]

==Economy==
Hawaii’s economy is dominated by tourism, government, real estate, and other service industries, and state analysts note that the economy is more concentrated than the U.S. economy overall.[2] In 2025 and early 2026, major indicators were mixed: wage and salary jobs, earnings, general excise tax revenues, visitor expenditures, and transient accommodation tax revenues rose, while visitor arrivals, private building authorizations, and some income tax revenues declined.[3] Visitor spending remained an important source of strength even when arrivals softened, with fourth-quarter 2025 air visitors down 3.2% year over year but visitor expenditures up 8.5%.[3] Labor market conditions also improved modestly, as the civilian labor force and non-agricultural wage and salary employment both increased in late 2025.[3]

The state also publishes detailed economic data and forecasts through the Research & Economic Analysis Division, which supports long-term planning and provides dashboards, data books, and research reports on Hawaii’s economy.[1] Recent state publications have covered topics such as tourism, international students, solar battery installations, targeted and emerging industries, film tax credits, social and business trends, and demographic groups within Hawaii.[1] Official labor force data show continued low unemployment and gradual employment growth into early 2026.[4]

==Culture==
Hawaii’s culture is the product of layered influences from Native Hawaiian traditions, colonial history, and the many immigrant communities that have settled in the islands.[1][4] Beginning in the 1970s, a Hawaiian cultural renaissance helped revive instruction in the Hawaiian language and renewed interest in traditional practices such as voyaging canoes, tattooing, kapa making, traditional music, and especially hula.[1] Many residents know at least some Hawaiian words and continue cultural customs such as giving lei.[1] The “Aloha Spirit” has also become a widely recognized expression of the way diverse groups live together in the islands.[1]  

Native Hawaiian culture was significantly diminished during the colonial period, particularly after the arrival of Western missionaries and businessmen, who discouraged the Hawaiian language and many traditional practices.[4] By the nineteenth century, outside influences had reshaped land ownership, politics, and the economy, contributing to the decline of Native Hawaiian cultural authority.[4] At the same time, scholarship on Hawaiian history and culture has emphasized the importance of understanding Native Hawaiians within their own ecological, historical, and cultural contexts.[3][4]  

Hawaii is also home to a range of museums and cultural institutions that preserve and interpret the islands’ heritage.[1] The Bernice P. Bishop Museum in Honolulu is dedicated to the study, preservation, and display of the history, sciences, and cultures of the Pacific and its peoples.[1] The Honolulu Academy of Arts, founded in 1927, holds major collections of Western and Asian art.[1]  

The traditions of many ethnic groups have become part of mainstream life in contemporary Hawaii, including the celebration of Chinese New Year and the annual Japanese Bon festival.[1] Hawaii’s cultural life is therefore often described as both distinctly Native Hawaiian and broadly multicultural.[1][5]

==Tourism==
Tourism is a major part of Hawaii’s economy and is described by the state as the activity most responsible for its current economic growth and standard of living. [2] The visitor industry also has broad effects beyond the economy, influencing infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life. [2] State tourism research has focused on understanding how tourism growth can be sustained while protecting social, economic, environmental, and physical infrastructure assets. [2]  

Hawaii’s tourism sector is closely tracked through a range of official data products, including visitor statistics, tourism forecasts, dashboards, and a tourism data warehouse. [1][2] These data sources are used to monitor trends such as visitor arrivals, spending, airline capacity, and daily passenger counts. [1][5]  

Tourism has significant economic benefits for Hawaii, including visitor spending, tax revenue, and employment support. [5] In calendar year 2024, visitor spending reached $20.68 billion, state tax revenue from tourism was $2.40 billion, and tourism supported about 213,000 jobs. [5] Visitor arrivals in 2024 totaled 9,657,607, showing a recovery from 2022 but still remaining below 2019 levels. [5]  

Air service is an important part of Hawaii’s tourism industry, and seat capacity is tracked closely by the state. [1][5] In the fourth quarter of 2025, total airline capacity to Hawaii increased slightly compared with the same quarter of 2024, with gains in both domestic and international seats. [1] For the full year 2025, however, total available seats declined compared with the previous year. [1]  

Tourism in Hawaii has also been the subject of studies on socio-cultural and environmental impacts. [2][4] These studies note concerns about visitor effects on crime, cultural values, social organization, and the distribution of costs and benefits between residents and the visitor industry. [4] More broadly, tourism has been linked to human-environment interaction and urbanization, including pressure on land use, natural landscapes, and cultural practices. [3]

==Health==
Hawaii’s health statistics and health planning are supported by a range of state, federal, and nonprofit data sources, including the Hawaii Department of Health, the CDC, the National Center for Health Statistics, Kaiser Family Foundation, and the Hawaii Health Information Corporation.[1] The state also maintains dedicated data resources such as the Hawaii Health Survey, the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse, and Hawaii Health Matters, which provide statewide and county-level information on health conditions, behaviors, and outcomes.[2][4]  

The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey of the health and socio-demographic conditions of Hawaii’s residents, designed to provide data for planning, evaluation, and health research.[4] Its core variables include gender, age, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and both mental and physical health status.[4]  

Public health data in Hawaii cover a wide variety of topics, including immunizations, infectious diseases, chronic disease indicators, and risk behaviors, with some datasets broken down by county or other local geographic levels.[2] The Hawaii Health Survey and related data systems are used to monitor population health and inform state health programs.[2][4]  

Health research on Hawaii also highlights the state’s healthcare workforce and the demand for health services, noting that employment, demographic change, and health trends may increase future healthcare needs.[3] Studies of Native Hawaiian health further emphasize the long historical context of health in the islands and the importance of understanding the needs of Native Hawaiians in modern health planning.[5]

==Education==
Hawaii’s public school system is statewide rather than divided among multiple districts, so it is not issued a school finance grade in the Quality Counts report card’s district-based analysis.[1] In that report, Hawaii ranked 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall grade of C.[1] The state received a B-minus in school spending, ranking 16th nationally, while its status score for current student performance was a D-minus and its change score for improvement over time was a C-minus.[1]

Culturally based education has also been a focus in Hawaii, including research on Hawaiian cultural influences in schooling and the use of Hawaiian language and other heritage materials in the classroom.[4] The Kamehameha Schools’ Hawaiian Cultural Influences in Education study was a statewide effort with the goal of understanding how to make education more engaging and relevant for Hawaiʻi’s children.[4]

Higher education in Hawaii is centered on the University of Hawaiʻi system, which includes three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the UH system in fall 2017 and accounted for 21.3% of degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]

Hawaii is also used as a setting for field-based and experiential learning, including Stanford Earth’s Wrigley Field Program, which brings undergraduates to the islands to study vegetation, coral reefs, volcanoes, and human-environment interactions.[3]

==Transportation==
Transportation accounts for about 55% of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up most of the demand.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in transportation is a central part of Hawaiʻi’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience.[5] State planning emphasizes both shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing total transportation energy demand.[5]  

The Hawaiʻi Clean Energy Initiative identified reducing vehicle miles traveled, expanding electric-drive vehicles, and adopting alternative fuels as key strategies for lowering transportation energy use.[1] In particular, rising vehicle miles traveled has been a major driver of ground transportation energy consumption in the state.[1]  

Electric vehicles are seen as an important way to cut transportation greenhouse gas emissions if they are powered by renewable energy.[3] Hawaiʻi has had relatively strong EV adoption compared with many other states, but EV ownership remains uneven across zip codes.[3] Research has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and more public charging infrastructure are associated with greater EV registration rates.[3]  

Because transportation electrification increases electricity demand, coordination between the energy and transportation systems is increasingly important.[5] Hawaiʻi’s high electricity costs and dependence on fossil fuels for power generation can affect the total cost and emissions benefits of EV ownership.[2] As a result, the value of grid-connected vehicles depends heavily on future assumptions about EV adoption, utility operations, and renewable energy growth.[2]

==Governance==
Hawaiʻi was originally settled by Polynesians and developed a distinct Native Hawaiian social and political system long before contact with the West. [1][3] Traditional governance was organized around ranked chiefs, with authority structured by genealogy, land divisions, and councils of chiefs. [1][4] Native Hawaiian sources describe this system as extending back many centuries, with ruling lineages traced through oral traditions and genealogies. [1][3]  

At the end of the 18th century, Kamehameha I united the islands and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi. [2] The kingdom was initially an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 Kamehameha III promulgated the first written constitution, transforming it into a constitutional monarchy. [2] Over time, successive constitutions reduced the monarch’s political powers, and Queen Liliʻuokalani was the last reigning monarch. [2]  

In the nineteenth century, political change also altered Native Hawaiian governance, including the increasing exclusion of women and, later, broader restrictions on voting. [4] Missionary influence and the adoption of Euro-American legal frameworks further reshaped the kingdom’s institutions and reduced Native Hawaiian political participation. [4]  

The Hawaiian monarchy was overthrown and replaced by the Republic of Hawaiʻi in 1894. [2] Hawaiʻi was annexed by the United States in 1898, became a U.S. territory in 1900, and was admitted as the 50th state in 1959. [2]  

Modern Hawaiian governance includes the office of the governor, who leads the executive branch of state government. [2] The Office of the Governor oversees numerous boards and commissions and handles communications, constituent services, and policy functions. [2]  

Native Hawaiian political history is also presented in contemporary scholarship as a continuous tradition of self-governance, distinct from the governance of the state’s multiethnic population. [3][5] These works emphasize that Native Hawaiians are the indigenous people of Hawaiʻi and that their forms of self-governance predate and persist alongside the modern state. [3][5]

==Hawaiian sovereignty movement==
The Hawaiian sovereignty movement is a broad political and cultural movement advocating self-governance, political independence, land stewardship, and the restoration or recognition of Hawaiian national identity.[1][3] Its modern activism is commonly traced to the early 1970s, when Native Hawaiian resistance and resurgence gained momentum through land struggles, eviction protests, and other grassroots initiatives.[1][5] The movement developed in response to the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the unresolved legal and political consequences that followed.[2][3][5]  

In its early decades, the movement included a range of organizations and strategies, from legal and diplomatic efforts in international forums to reparations-based advocacy and community organizing.[2][3][5] Some groups emphasized the illegality of the overthrow and sought decolonization, while others focused on cultural revitalization, land rights, and the protection of Native Hawaiian communities.[1][2][4] Scholars have noted that by the late 1980s and early 1990s, sovereignty had shifted from being seen as a marginal idea to a more widely supported political position among Native Hawaiians.[4]  

Key issues within the movement include whether sovereignty, self-governance, or independence are feasible goals, and what forms of political restoration are most realistic or supported by the public and the United States government.[2][5] The movement also reflects differing understandings of Hawaiian history and political status, with some advocates viewing the Hawaiian Kingdom as a sovereign state whose sovereignty was never lawfully extinguished.[3][5] The broader movement continues to encompass both legal and political approaches as well as efforts to revive Hawaiian identity and strengthen lāhui and aloha ʻāina traditions.[1][3]

==International sister relationships==
Hawaiʻi has developed a Sister-State program through the Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism to build long-term partnerships with foreign governments and organizations that promote cultural, environmental, and economic ties.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2 and is supported by the governor, legislature, and the Office of International Affairs.[1]  

As of the most recent report, Hawaiʻi has 19 sister relationships.[2] These include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993) in Taiwan.[1][2]  

The state’s policy emphasizes that successful sister relationships should have commonality with Hawaiʻi, especially in geographical, historical, cultural, or economic terms, and that proposed relationships are reviewed through a formal application and evaluation process.[2] The committee also periodically reviews existing relationships to determine which should be strengthened or severed.[2]  

Several of Hawaiʻi’s sister relationships have been especially active, including those with Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong.[2][3] Hawaiʻi’s ties with Fukuoka have also been highlighted through cultural exchange efforts and commemorative activities involving the Hawaiʻi Global Education Foundation and related organizations.[3]  

At the county level, Hawaiʻi County has also developed sister city relationships in the Indo-Pacific region, with particularly strong ties to Japan, including Hatsukaichi, a partnership grounded in shared natural beauty, cultural heritage, and family connections.[4]

Hawaii consists of eight major islands and 124 minor islands, with a total land area of 6,425 square miles and a general coastline of about 750 miles.[2][4] The major islands, from west to east, are Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, Maui, and Hawaii.[5] The state extends across a long volcanic island chain in the central Pacific Ocean, stretching roughly 1,500 miles from Kure Island in the west to the island of Hawaii in the east.[5] Honolulu is 214 miles from Hilo, 1,367 miles from Kure Atoll, 2,397 miles from San Francisco, and 4,829 miles from Washington, D.C.[2][4] The highest peak in the state is Mauna Kea, which rises 13,796 feet above sea level.[2][4] Other major geographic features include Kaukonahua Stream on Oahu, the state’s longest stream at 33 miles, Halalii on Niihau, the largest lake at 841 acres, and Kahiwa Falls on Molokai, the highest named waterfall at 1,750 feet.[2][4]

Much of the Hawaiian landscape was formed by volcanic activity as the Pacific Plate moved over a hotspot beneath the central Pacific Ocean.[5] The islands and seamounts of the chain began forming at least 70 million years ago, and volcanic activity remains active today at Mauna Loa and Kilauea on Hawaii, as well as at the Lō‘ihi Seamount southeast of the island.[5] Hawaii’s highest mountains are on the island of Hawaii, and the state’s topography includes dramatic sea cliffs, especially along the northeastern coast of Molokai.[4][5] The cliffs between Umilehi Point and Puukaoku Point rise to about 3,250 feet and have been described as among the highest sea cliffs in the world.[4]

The Hawaiian sovereignty movement is a broad political and cultural movement advocating self-governance, political independence, land stewardship, and the restoration or recognition of Hawaiian national identity.[1][3] Its modern activism is commonly traced to the early 1970s, when Native Hawaiian resistance and resurgence gained momentum through land struggles, eviction protests, and other grassroots initiatives.[1][5] The movement developed in response to the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom and the unresolved legal and political consequences that followed.[2][3][5]  

In its early decades, the movement included a range of organizations and strategies, from legal and diplomatic efforts in international forums to reparations-based advocacy and community organizing.[2][3][5] Some groups emphasized the illegality of the overthrow and sought decolonization, while others focused on cultural revitalization, land rights, and the protection of Native Hawaiian communities.[1][2][4] Scholars have noted that by the late 1980s and early 1990s, sovereignty had shifted from being seen as a marginal idea to a more widely supported political position among Native Hawaiians.[4]  

Key issues within the movement include whether sovereignty, self-governance, or independence are feasible goals, and what forms of political restoration are most realistic or supported by the public and the United States government.[2][5] The movement also reflects differing understandings of Hawaiian history and political status, with some advocates viewing the Hawaiian Kingdom as a sovereign state whose sovereignty was never lawfully extinguished.[3][5] The broader movement continues to encompass both legal and political approaches as well as efforts to revive Hawaiian identity and strengthen lāhui and aloha ʻāina traditions.[1][3]

Hawaiʻi has developed a Sister-State program through the Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism to build long-term partnerships with foreign governments and organizations that promote cultural, environmental, and economic ties.[1] The program is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2 and is supported by the governor, legislature, and the Office of International Affairs.[1]  

As of the most recent report, Hawaiʻi has 19 sister relationships.[2] These include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993) in Taiwan.[1][2]  

The state’s policy emphasizes that successful sister relationships should have commonality with Hawaiʻi, especially in geographical, historical, cultural, or economic terms, and that proposed relationships are reviewed through a formal application and evaluation process.[2] The committee also periodically reviews existing relationships to determine which should be strengthened or severed.[2]  

Several of Hawaiʻi’s sister relationships have been especially active, including those with Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong.[2][3] Hawaiʻi’s ties with Fukuoka have also been highlighted through cultural exchange efforts and commemorative activities involving the Hawaiʻi Global Education Foundation and related organizations.[3]  

At the county level, Hawaiʻi County has also developed sister city relationships in the Indo-Pacific region, with particularly strong ties to Japan, including Hatsukaichi, a partnership grounded in shared natural beauty, cultural heritage, and family connections.[4]

==Geography and environment==

The climate and environment of Hawaiʻi vary greatly by location, with rainfall ranging from very low totals in some coastal areas to extremely wet conditions on windward mountain slopes.[4] The state has also experienced notable natural hazards, including long volcanic eruptions, major earthquakes, and large tsunami waves.[4] Water use and environmental conditions have been monitored closely, with statewide water withdrawals averaging 2.7 billion gallons per day in 1980.[4] Air and water quality, along with other environmental measures, have long been tracked by state and federal agencies and by the University of Hawaiʻi.[1][4]

==History==

During the late 18th and early 19th centuries, Kamehameha I expanded his power through a series of battles, including Nuʻuanu in 1795, and unified the Hawaiian Islands by 1810.[3] The Kamehameha dynasty then ruled the kingdom from 1795 to 1874.[3] In 1819, Liholiho’s public defiance of the traditional kapu separation of men and women at a feast helped bring about the abolition of the kapu system.[3] The first Protestant missionaries arrived in 1820, and Lahaina served as the capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from 1820 to 1845.[3]

In the mid-19th century, plantation agriculture became a major force in Hawaiian life, with the first sugar plantation opening on Kauaʻi in 1835.[3] Hawaiʻi also became increasingly shaped by immigration and labor recruitment, including Japanese emigration companies, Russian settlement efforts, Chinese contract-labor schemes, and a growing missionary and religious presence.[1] These changes contributed to major social and linguistic developments in the islands, as Hawaiʻi became a multiethnic society with distinct regional and social histories.[1][5]

Hawaiʻi became a constituent state of the United States on August 21, 1959.[4] Its modern history continues to be marked by the preservation of historic sites, cultural traditions, and the study of its indigenous past.[1][3]

==Demographics==
Hawaii’s population has been around 1.4 million in recent years, with the U.S. Census Bureau estimating 1,432,820 residents in July 2025 and 1,446,146 in July 2024.[5] The state’s 2021 population was estimated at 1,441,553, and Oʻahu accounted for about 69.4% of residents, making the island by far the most populous part of the state.[1] In the 2020 census, Hawaii had a population of 1,455,271, up from 1,360,301 in 2010.[5]  

Hawaii has a highly diverse racial and ethnic composition, with no single group forming a majority of the population.[5] According to 2020–2024 census estimates, Asians made up 37.4% of the population, two or more races 24.5%, White residents 25.1%, Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islanders 10.4%, Black residents 2.2%, American Indian and Alaska Native residents 0.4%, and Hispanic or Latino residents 10.2%.[5] Earlier census data also showed especially large populations of Filipino, Japanese, Native Hawaiian, and Chinese residents.[3]  

Native Hawaiians remain a central part of the state’s demographic profile.[4] One analysis described Native Hawaiians as about 6% of Hawaii’s population, or about 21% when part-Hawaiian residents are included.[4]  

Immigration has also shaped Hawaii’s population, reflecting the state’s role as a gateway to the United States from Asia and the Pacific.[1] Based on the 2021 American Community Survey, Hawaii had 270,345 immigrants, or 18.8% of its residents, the sixth-highest immigrant share among the states.[1]  

Age and household characteristics in Hawaii are somewhat older than the national average, with 21.5% of residents age 65 and over and 20.3% under age 18 in the 2020–2024 estimates.[5] In the same period, 49.9% of residents were female, 93.2% of adults had at least a high school diploma, and 36.1% held a bachelor’s degree or higher.[5]

==Economy==
Hawaiʻi’s economy is shaped by tourism, public-sector employment, trade, real estate, and a range of service industries, and the state’s economic agency regularly publishes data, forecasts, and sector-specific reports to support planning and analysis.[1] The state also maintains economic dashboards, data warehouses, and the Hawaiʻi State Data Center as official resources for researchers and the public.[1]

Tourism remains one of the most closely watched parts of the economy: state reports track visitor arrivals, expenditures, and related tax revenues, reflecting the sector’s importance to overall economic performance.[3] In the fourth quarter of 2025, visitor arrivals by air declined while visitor expenditures increased, showing that higher spending can offset weaker arrivals in some periods.[3]

Labor market conditions have generally improved, with the civilian labor force and non-agricultural wage and salary employment both increasing in late 2025.[3] Monthly labor force data for early 2026 show unemployment remaining low, with the statewide unemployment rate around the low 2 percent range and total nonfarm employment holding near 643,000 jobs.[4]

Hawaiʻi’s gross domestic product is relatively concentrated in a small number of industry groups compared with the United States as a whole.[2] In 2020, the top five industry groups accounted for a larger share of total GDP in Hawaiʻi than they did nationally, with government, real estate and rental and leasing, retail trade, construction, and professional, scientific, and technical services among the major contributors in the state.[2]

Overall real GDP growth in Hawaiʻi was modest over the 2010s, and the economy was affected by a sharp decline in 2020 associated with the COVID-19 pandemic.[2] State analysis notes that the 2010 to 2020 increase in total real GDP was small because of the 2019 to 2020 contraction.[2]

The state continues to publish research on population, industry structure, income needs, film incentives, and other economic topics, indicating ongoing attention to structural change and policy evaluation.[1]

==Culture==
Hawaiian culture is the product of successive layers of influence from Native Hawaiian traditions and later immigrant communities, and colonialism long weakened many aspects of Indigenous culture before a revival began in the 1970s.[1] This renaissance renewed emphasis on the Hawaiian language and on traditional practices such as voyaging canoes, tattooing, music, kapa making, and especially hula.[1] Many residents use Hawaiian words and observe customs such as giving the lei, while the “Aloha Spirit” is often described as reflecting the way diverse communities live together in the islands.[1]

Hawaii is also home to important cultural institutions that preserve and interpret the history and arts of the Pacific.[1] The Bernice P. Bishop Museum in Honolulu was founded in 1889 as a research center and museum devoted to the history, sciences, and cultures of the Pacific and its peoples.[1] The Honolulu Academy of Arts, founded in 1927, holds notable collections of Western and Asian art.[1]

Traditional Hawaiian culture developed long before Western contact and included distinctive settlement patterns, house styles, material culture, subsistence practices, and social, political, and religious organization.[3] By the time of sustained Western contact, Hawaiian cultural traditions were well established, though they were later disrupted by colonial forces, missionary activity, and the decline of Native Hawaiian political power.[4] Some scholars have argued that major cultural changes in Hawaiian society were also shaped by deliberate action by Queen Kaʻahumanu, who used political and symbolic change to pursue power.[2]

Today, many traditions once marginalized have become part of mainstream life in Hawaii, including celebrations such as Chinese New Year and the Japanese Bon festival.[1] Hawaii’s cultural heritage continues to be presented in ways that emphasize both historical continuity and the ongoing importance of living traditions.[5]

==Tourism==
Tourism is a major driver of Hawaiʻi’s economy and is described by the state’s Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism as the activity most responsible for current economic growth and standard of living.[2] At the same time, the visitor industry has wide-ranging effects on Hawaiʻi’s economy, physical infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life.[2] State studies have therefore examined tourism not only as an economic sector but also as an issue of sustainability, focusing on whether the benefits of visitor activity can be maintained while protecting environmental and social assets.[2][4]  

Recent state reporting shows that tourism has continued to recover and fluctuate in the post-pandemic period, with visitor spending reaching $20.68 billion in 2024 and visitor arrivals totaling 9,657,607 in 2023, while both figures remained below 2019 levels in some measures.[5] Air service remains a key indicator of tourism capacity, and the state reported 59,725 transpacific flights with 13,118,442 seats in 2023, alongside continued changes in seat capacity by quarter and by market segment.[5][1]  

Hawaiʻi’s tourism analysis also emphasizes socio-cultural impacts, including the effects of tourism on local communities, cultural traditions, and perceptions of crime and safety.[4] Researchers have noted that tourism can create tensions over the distribution of costs and benefits, as well as questions involving cultural values, social organization, and self-esteem.[4] In broader course-based discussions of Hawaiʻi, tourism is often linked to human-environment interaction and urbanization, especially where visitor demand contributes to land development and environmental strain.[3]

==Health==
Hawaii’s health data and public-health reporting are coordinated primarily through the Hawaii State Department of Health, often in collaboration with federal and nonprofit partners such as the CDC and the National Center for Health Statistics.[1] The state maintains multiple health-data resources, including the Hawaii Health Survey, the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse, and Hawaii Health Matters, which provide statewide and county-level indicators on topics such as immunization, infectious disease, chronic disease, and behavioral risk factors.[2][4]

The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey of health and sociodemographic conditions in the islands’ population.[4] It is used by the Department of Health and other agencies to support planning, evaluation, and health research, and it collects core variables such as age, income, ethnicity, education, insurance status, and mental and physical health status.[4]

Available state health indicators show a broad interest in measuring conditions affecting different groups, including women, older adults, teenagers, and Native Hawaiians.[1][3] Publicly available datasets also include information on reportable infectious diseases, crime, and school performance, reflecting a wider effort to link health outcomes with social conditions.[2]

Historical and scholarly work on health in Hawaii emphasizes the distinct health experience of Native Hawaiians and the long-term demographic and epidemiological changes affecting the population.[5] These studies have highlighted the decline of the Native Hawaiian population after Western contact and have been used to inform culturally appropriate care and health promotion efforts.[5]

More recent analyses suggest continuing concern about chronic disease, obesity, and mental health, especially among adolescents and older adults, and note that demand for health care services in Hawaii is likely to rise as the population grows and ages.[3]

==Education==
Education in Hawaii is administered by a single statewide public school district, which means the state is not issued a school-finance grade in analyses that compare funding distribution across districts.[1] In Education Week’s *Quality Counts* report card, Hawaii’s school-finance spending indicators were rated B-minus, ranking it 16th nationally and above the national average of D.[1] The same report described Hawaii’s overall education performance as 22nd among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall grade of C.[1] Hawaii’s student achievement status was rated D-minus, placing the state 41st in the nation, while its year-to-year achievement change was rated C-minus, ranking 10th.[1]  

The University of Hawaiʻi system includes three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers across six islands.[5] Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in the system in fall 2017 and received 21.3% of degrees and certificates in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]  

Hawaii has also been a focus of research into culturally based and Indigenous education, including studies of Hawaiian language use, heritage-language materials, and community participation in teaching.[4] Kamehameha Schools’ Hawaiian Cultural Influences in Education project described statewide research intended to better understand how to make education more engaging and relevant for Hawaiʻi’s children.[4] Field-based learning has also been highlighted in the state, with Stanford University’s Wrigley Field Program in Hawaii using the islands as a living classroom for studies of marine biology, volcanology, anthropology, and environmental management.[3]

==Transportation==
Transportation accounts for approximately 55 percent of Hawaiʻi’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation responsible for much of the demand.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence in this sector is a central goal of the state’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience efforts.[5] State energy analyses emphasize two main approaches: shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing total transportation energy demand.[1][5]  

Hawaiʻi’s transportation planning also includes strategies to reduce vehicle miles traveled, expand electric-drive vehicles, and promote alternative fuels as part of a broader clean-energy roadmap.[1] Because the state’s islands rely heavily on imported goods, maritime transportation is also important to Hawaiʻi’s economy and energy system.[5] Marine fuel use contributes to the transportation sector’s petroleum consumption, while domestic marine travel is included in the state greenhouse gas inventory and international bunker fuel emissions are reported separately.[5]  

Electric vehicles are seen as a major opportunity to lower greenhouse gas emissions in Hawaiʻi, especially if they are powered by renewable energy.[3] Research on EV adoption in the state has found that higher income, higher educational attainment, and greater charging infrastructure are associated with more EV registrations.[3] However, Hawaiʻi’s high electricity costs, continued reliance on fossil-fueled generation, and the technical challenges of small isolated island grids complicate the integration of large numbers of grid-connected vehicles.[2]

==Governance==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, probably from the Marquesas, during a colonization period beginning around AD 300 to 600, and over the following centuries a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, and social system developed.[1] Native Hawaiian governance is described as having emerged through ruling chiefs whose genealogies, in some traditions, are traced back many generations before the formation of the modern Hawaiian Kingdom.[1][3]

By the late 18th century, the Hawaiian Islands were ruled by various chiefs until they were unified by Kamehameha the Great, who established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[2] The kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 King Kamehameha III signed Hawaiʻi’s first written constitution, transforming the government into a constitutional monarchy.[2] Under later constitutions, the monarch’s political power was increasingly limited, and men became the primary political actors as voting and office-holding were restricted in ways that gradually excluded many Native Hawaiians and others from participation.[2][4]

Hawaiian political authority had long been organized through a ranked chiefly system in which aliʻi nui, kaukau aliʻi, and konohiki exercised responsibilities over different land and district units, while councils such as the ʻaha aliʻi provided advice to rulers.[4] In the nineteenth century, missionary influence and the adoption of Euro-American legal frameworks further reshaped governance, including laws based on Christian moral standards and franchise rules tied to property ownership, taxation, and literacy.[4]

The monarchy ended after the overthrow of Queen Liliʻuokalani, and Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894.[2] Sovereignty was transferred to the United States in 1898, Hawaiʻi became a U.S. territory in 1900, and it was admitted as the 50th state on August 21, 1959.[2]

Modern discussions of governance in Hawaiʻi also include Native Hawaiian claims to self-governance, with some accounts emphasizing the continuity of indigenous political identity and sovereignty from precontact times to the present.[3][5] The State of Hawaiʻi government is headed by the governor, whose office oversees boards, commissions, communications, constituent services, and policy functions.[2]

==Hawaiian sovereignty movement==
The Hawaiian sovereignty movement is a broad political and cultural movement advocating Native Hawaiian self-determination, autonomy, or full restoration of an independent Hawaiian nation.[1][2][3] It emerged in the 1970s and expanded over subsequent decades through grassroots activism, legal challenges, cultural revitalization, and land- and resource-based struggles.[1][4][5] Scholars and observers have described it as one of the most significant catalysts for political change in Hawaiʻi, particularly from the late 1980s into the early 1990s.[4]  

The movement draws much of its historical justification from the overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom in 1893, which supporters argue left unresolved questions of sovereignty, international recognition, and political legitimacy.[3][5] It is also shaped by the idea that Native Hawaiians lost both internal governance rights and external sovereignty when the kingdom was overthrown.[5] In this view, sovereignty is not only a symbolic or cultural issue but also one involving treaty history, land title, governance authority, and the political status of the Hawaiian people.[3]  

Organizations within the movement have pursued different strategies and goals, including international legal remedies, reparations, cultural resurgence, and the restoration of a Hawaiian nation-state.[1][2][3] Some groups have focused on decolonization and recognition in international forums, while others have emphasized land stewardship, community organizing, and the protection of Native Hawaiian political rights.[1][3] The movement has also encompassed varied perspectives on whether sovereignty should mean independence, self-governance within the United States, or another form of political restoration.[2][5]  

Major issues associated with the movement include the 1893 overthrow, the U.S. annexation of Hawaiʻi, land dispossession, military occupation and land use, and the long-term consequences of colonization for Native Hawaiian identity and governance.[1][4][5] Land struggles such as those at Kalama Valley, Waiāhole/Waiākane, Hilo Airport, and Kahoʻolawe are often cited as important episodes in the movement’s history.[1] Native Hawaiian women have also played prominent roles in sovereignty activism and demilitarization efforts.[1]

==International sister relationships==
Hawaiʻi maintains a formal sister-state program through the state Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism (DBEDT), which says the relationships are intended to strengthen Hawaiʻi’s international profile, encourage cooperative programs, and support the state’s economy.[1] The program was shaped by a policy adopted in 1993 that made economic benefit a co-equal goal alongside friendship, cultural ties, and goodwill, and it is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes, Chapter 229-2, which establishes the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission.[1] The commission is tasked with supporting the governor, legislature, and office of international affairs in maintaining long-term partnerships that promote the peoples, cultures, environments, and economies of Hawaiʻi and its international partners.[1]  

DBEDT’s sister-state criteria emphasize commonality between Hawaiʻi and the prospective sister, especially in geographical, historical, cultural, or economic terms, and the process for approval includes a governor’s letter, a designated point of contact, recent activity reports, and recommendations for future activities.[2] The state periodically reviews these relationships and has stated that Hawaiʻi has 19 sister relationships dating back to 1981, with some ties described as especially active, including Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong.[2]  

As of the most recent report, Hawaiʻi’s sister-state and sister-province relationships include Guangdong and Hainan in China; Bali in Indonesia; Fukuoka, Okinawa, Hiroshima, Ehime, Hokkaido, and Yamaguchi in Japan; Jeju in South Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer in Morocco; Ilocos Sur, Cebu, Pangasinan, Ilocos Norte, and Isabela in the Philippines; the Azores Islands in Portugal; and Taiwan.[1] A separate report also notes that Hawaiʻi has sister-state and friendship relationships with 18 states or prefectures worldwide and highlights especially long-standing ties with several Japanese prefectures.[3]
